concepts.name: Array
concepts.definition: a contiguous block of memory storing elements of the same type, accessible via integer indices.
concepts.analogy: a row of numbered mailboxes — each slot has a fixed address
concepts.basic_ops: access by index, iterate, update element
concepts.internal_layout: contiguously in a single heap allocation; element i is at base_address + i * element_size
concepts.memory_note: statically or dynamically at initialization; resizing requires a full copy
concepts.struct_props: fixed size, O(1) random access, cache-friendly sequential layout
concepts.invariants: indices are zero-based; all elements share the same type and size; memory is contiguous
concepts.theoretical_note: Provides the tightest possible cache locality; underpins most higher-level structures
concepts.ops_basic:
- Access: O(1)
  - Search (unsorted): O(n)
  - Insert/Delete (end): O(1)
  - Insert/Delete (middle): O(n)
concepts.ops_full:
- Access: O(1) avg & worst
  - Search: O(n) unsorted, O(log n) sorted (binary search)
  - Insert/Delete at end: O(1) amortized
  - Insert/Delete at index: O(n)
concepts.ops_advanced:
- Access: O(1) via direct pointer arithmetic
  - Dynamic resize: O(n) copy but O(1) amortized per push via doubling
  - Cache miss rate: minimal due to spatial locality
concepts.complexity_note: Sorted arrays unlock O(log n) search but make insertion O(n)
concepts.amortized_note: Doubling strategy keeps amortized push O(1): total copies after n inserts is at most 2n
concepts.beginner_use_case: storing a list of student scores
concepts.beginner_reason: scores are accessed by position and the count is known in advance
concepts.applications: image pixel buffers, matrix math, lookup tables, sorting algorithms
concepts.intermediate_reason: O(1) access and cache locality make it ideal for read-heavy, index-driven workloads
concepts.advanced_scenario: SIMD vectorization is needed and memory layout must be predictable
concepts.advanced_reason: contiguous layout allows hardware prefetching and vectorized operations unavailable with pointer-chased structures
concepts.real_world: NumPy ndarray, GPU tensor buffers, ring buffers in network drivers
concepts.advantages:
O(1) random access
Cache-friendly memory layout
Simple and low overhead
concepts.disadvantages:
Fixed size (static arrays)
O(n) insertion/deletion in the middle
Wasted space if over-allocated
concepts.prefer_this: frequent index-based access or iteration is needed
concepts.prefer_alt: frequent insertions/deletions at arbitrary positions dominate
concepts.key_diff: Arrays trade insertion flexibility for access speed; linked lists do the opposite
concepts.limitations: resizing is expensive; middle insertions shift all subsequent elements; no O(1) insert at arbitrary position
concepts.mitigations: gap buffers (text editors), tiered arrays, using ArrayList/vector with amortized doubling
concepts.prod_example: CPython list uses a dynamic array with 1.125x over-allocation to amortize appends

concepts.name: Dynamic Array
concepts.definition: a resizable array that automatically grows by allocating a larger block and copying elements when capacity is exceeded.
concepts.analogy: a suitcase that doubles in size each time it gets full
concepts.basic_ops: append, access by index, pop, insert
concepts.internal_layout: contiguously; capacity is tracked separately from length, with unused slots at the end
concepts.memory_note: on the heap; a new allocation 2x the old size is made on overflow, then elements are copied
concepts.struct_props: amortized O(1) append, O(1) random access, O(n) worst-case resize
concepts.invariants: length <= capacity; capacity doubles on overflow; shrinking is optional
concepts.theoretical_note: The doubling invariant guarantees amortized O(1) append via potential-function argument
concepts.ops_basic:
- Access: O(1)
  - Append: O(1) amortized
  - Insert/Delete middle: O(n)
  - Search: O(n)
concepts.ops_full:
- Access: O(1)
  - Append: O(1) amortized, O(n) worst case
  - Insert at index k: O(n-k)
  - Pop last: O(1)
  - Search: O(n) unsorted
concepts.ops_advanced:
- Amortized append: O(1) by doubling — total cost for n appends is O(n)
  - Shrink threshold at 1/4 capacity avoids thrashing at the boundary
  - Insert at k: O(n) shift — mitigated by gap-buffer or rope variants
concepts.space_complexity: O(n) with up to 2x wasted capacity
concepts.complexity_note: Worst-case append is O(n) but occurs at most once every n ops; amortized cost is O(1)
concepts.amortized_note: Potential Phi = 2*size - capacity; each append pays O(1) real + amortized cost keeping Phi non-negative
concepts.beginner_use_case: building a list of user inputs when the final count is unknown
concepts.beginner_reason: you don't need to pre-specify size, and appending is fast
concepts.applications: Python list, Java ArrayList, C++ std::vector, stack implementations
concepts.intermediate_reason: combines random access of arrays with flexible sizing, covering most general-purpose list needs
concepts.advanced_scenario: append-heavy workloads that also need O(1) indexed reads (e.g., event logs, append-only stores)
concepts.advanced_reason: amortized O(1) append + O(1) read beats linked lists on both cache and time for sequential workloads
concepts.real_world: CPython list, Java ArrayList growth policy, Rust Vec<T>
concepts.advantages:
Amortized O(1) append
O(1) random access
No fixed size limit
concepts.disadvantages:
Up to 2x memory waste
O(n) worst-case resize
O(n) middle insert/delete
concepts.prefer_this: indexed access or iteration performance is critical
concepts.prefer_alt: frequent mid-list insertions/deletions are needed
concepts.key_diff: Dynamic array wins on read and cache; linked list wins on arbitrary insert
concepts.limitations: memory spikes during resize; fragmentation if many small arrays exist; middle insert is O(n)
concepts.mitigations: pre-reserve capacity when final size is estimable; use deque for O(1) front inserts
concepts.prod_example: Rust's Vec doubles capacity and uses raw pointer arithmetic for zero-overhead indexing

concepts.name: Circular Buffer
concepts.definition: a fixed-size array used as a queue by wrapping head and tail pointers around using modular arithmetic.
concepts.analogy: a conveyor belt in a factory — items enter one end and leave the other, and the belt loops
concepts.internal_layout: a fixed array plus two integer pointers (head, tail) and a count or full flag
concepts.memory_note: allocated once at initialization; no dynamic resizing — size is fixed at construction
concepts.struct_props: O(1) enqueue/dequeue, O(1) space per op, no memory allocation after init
concepts.invariants: 0 <= head, tail < capacity; next index = (current + 1) % capacity; full when count == capacity
concepts.theoretical_note: Achieves O(1) amortized throughput with zero allocation overhead, ideal for real-time systems
concepts.ops_basic:
- Enqueue: O(1)
  - Dequeue: O(1)
  - Peek: O(1)
  - Search: O(n)
concepts.ops_full:
- Enqueue: O(1)
  - Dequeue: O(1)
  - Peek/front: O(1)
  - Full/empty check: O(1)
  - Indexed access: O(1) with offset math
concepts.ops_advanced:
- All core ops: O(1) with no heap allocation
  - Throughput bounded only by memory bandwidth
  - Lock-free variants use CAS on head/tail for single-producer/single-consumer (SPSC)
concepts.complexity_note: Fixed capacity means no resize cost but overflow must be handled explicitly (drop or block)
concepts.amortized_note: No amortization needed — all ops are strictly O(1) with no reallocation
concepts.beginner_use_case: storing the last 10 log messages to display on a console
concepts.beginner_reason: old entries are automatically overwritten when the buffer is full, no manual cleanup needed
concepts.applications: audio/video streaming buffers, OS kernel I/O ring buffers, network packet queues, producer-consumer pipelines
concepts.intermediate_reason: zero-allocation O(1) ops make it perfect for latency-sensitive or interrupt-driven systems
concepts.advanced_scenario: a real-time audio engine needs a lock-free pipeline between producer and consumer threads
concepts.advanced_reason: SPSC circular buffer with memory-order fences achieves wait-free behavior without locks
concepts.real_world: Linux kernel kfifo, io_uring submission/completion queues, JACK audio ring buffer
concepts.advantages:
O(1) enqueue and dequeue
No dynamic allocation after init
Cache-friendly layout
concepts.disadvantages:
Fixed capacity
Overflow handling is manual
Not suitable for variable-length items without extra indirection
concepts.prefer_this: throughput and latency predictability matter; size is bounded and known
concepts.prefer_alt: queue size is unbounded or items have variable lifetimes
concepts.key_diff: Circular buffer is allocation-free and cache-friendly; linked list queue supports unbounded growth
concepts.limitations: fixed size requires capacity planning; overflow policy (drop/block/overwrite) must be chosen at design time
concepts.mitigations: double-capacity on overflow for soft real-time; use backpressure signaling in streaming pipelines
concepts.prod_example: Linux io_uring uses a pair of circular buffers (SQ/CQ) for zero-syscall I/O submission

concepts.name: Sparse Array
concepts.definition: a data structure that efficiently stores arrays where most elements are zero or a default value, using only space for non-default entries.
concepts.analogy: a phone book that only lists people who actually exist, not every possible name
concepts.basic_ops: get(i), set(i, v), iterate non-zero entries
concepts.internal_layout: as a hash map of index->value pairs, or as sorted arrays of (index, value) pairs (CSR/COO formats)
concepts.memory_note: only non-default entries consume memory; O(k) where k is the number of non-zero elements
concepts.struct_props: O(k) space, O(1) average get/set via hashing, O(k log k) sorted access
concepts.invariants: absent keys implicitly map to the default value (usually 0); stored entries are non-default
concepts.theoretical_note: For density d much less than 1, sparse representation gives O(d*n^2) vs O(n^2) for dense
concepts.ops_basic:
- Get: O(1) avg
  - Set: O(1) avg
  - Iterate non-zeros: O(k)
concepts.ops_full:
- Get: O(1) avg hash / O(log k) sorted
  - Set: O(1) avg hash / O(log k) sorted
  - Row/column slice: O(k)
  - Matrix-vector multiply: O(k)
concepts.ops_advanced:
- SpMV (sparse matrix-vector multiply): O(nnz) vs O(n^2) dense
  - CSR format enables cache-efficient row access
  - Conversion CSR<->COO: O(k) but invalidates iterators
concepts.space_complexity: O(k) where k = number of non-zero elements
concepts.complexity_note: Break-even with dense array at ~30-50% density; below that, sparse wins on both space and compute
concepts.amortized_note: Hash-backed sparse array has O(1) amortized set due to load-factor resizing of the underlying hash map
concepts.beginner_use_case: storing grades for students who only submitted some assignments
concepts.beginner_reason: most entries are missing (default 0), so only storing actual grades saves memory
concepts.applications: scientific computing (FEM matrices), graph adjacency matrices, NLP term-document matrices, game world maps
concepts.intermediate_reason: most real-world matrices (social graphs, web link graphs) are over 99% zero; dense storage would be infeasible
concepts.advanced_scenario: training a recommender system where the user-item rating matrix has less than 0.1% density
concepts.advanced_reason: sparse formats reduce memory by 1000x and enable vectorized SpMV, making gradient computation tractable
concepts.real_world: SciPy CSR matrix, TensorFlow SparseTensor, PyTorch sparse_coo_tensor
concepts.advantages:
O(k) memory — huge savings for low-density data
Fast iteration over non-zeros
Enables feasible computation on otherwise huge matrices
concepts.disadvantages:
Higher per-element overhead than dense
Random access slower than dense array
Complex to implement efficiently
concepts.prefer_this: data is less than 10-30% non-zero
concepts.prefer_alt: data is dense or random access patterns are unpredictable
concepts.key_diff: Sparse saves memory and computation for low-density data; dense wins on simplicity and random access speed
concepts.limitations: hash-based sparse arrays have poor cache behavior; format conversion (COO<->CSR) is expensive
concepts.mitigations: use CSR for row-access patterns, CSC for column-access; choose format at construction based on access pattern
concepts.prod_example: Google's PageRank operates on a sparse adjacency matrix with billions of rows but only ~10 non-zeros per row

concepts.name: Singly Linked List
concepts.definition: a sequence of nodes where each node holds a value and a pointer to the next node, with the last node pointing to null.
concepts.analogy: a treasure hunt where each clue tells you where the next clue is hidden
concepts.basic_ops: insert at head, remove head, traverse
concepts.internal_layout: as scattered heap nodes; each node = {value, *next}; no contiguous memory
concepts.memory_note: dynamically on the heap per node; pointer overhead adds 8 bytes per node on 64-bit systems
concepts.struct_props: O(1) head insert/delete, O(n) traversal, no random access
concepts.invariants: head points to first node or null; last node's next is null; length tracked separately or computed in O(n)
concepts.theoretical_note: Optimal for prepend-heavy workloads; poor cache locality due to pointer chasing
concepts.ops_basic:
- Insert at head: O(1)
  - Delete head: O(1)
  - Search: O(n)
  - Insert at tail: O(n)
concepts.ops_full:
- Insert/delete at head: O(1)
  - Insert/delete at tail: O(n) without tail pointer, O(1) with
  - Search by value: O(n)
  - Access by index: O(n)
concepts.ops_advanced:
- All operations are pointer-chase bound: cache miss per node hop
  - Memory locality: worst case (each node separately allocated)
  - Lock-free variant uses CAS on head pointer for concurrent prepend
concepts.complexity_note: Tail insert is O(n) without a tail pointer; adding one reduces it to O(1) at the cost of bookkeeping
concepts.amortized_note: No amortized behavior — all ops are exact; no resizing or batching occurs
concepts.beginner_use_case: implementing an undo history where only the most recent action needs fast access
concepts.beginner_reason: new actions are prepended in O(1) and undo pops the head in O(1)
concepts.applications: undo stacks, symbol tables in compilers, adjacency lists, OS free block lists
concepts.intermediate_reason: O(1) prepend and splice make it ideal for structures that grow at the front or need O(1) merge
concepts.advanced_scenario: a memory allocator needs to track a free list with O(1) alloc and free
concepts.advanced_reason: free blocks are linked directly using the block's own memory for the next pointer — zero overhead
concepts.real_world: Linux kernel free_list, CPython's small object allocator free lists, Lisp cons cells
concepts.advantages:
O(1) insert/delete at head
Dynamic size
No wasted capacity
concepts.disadvantages:
O(n) search and indexed access
Poor cache locality
Extra memory for pointers
concepts.prefer_this: frequent O(1) insertions/deletions at the front are needed
concepts.prefer_alt: random access or cache performance is critical
concepts.key_diff: Linked list excels at head mutations; array excels at indexed access and cache efficiency
concepts.limitations: pointer chasing causes cache misses; no O(1) tail access without extra pointer; reverse traversal impossible
concepts.mitigations: maintain explicit tail pointer; use memory pools to improve cache locality of nodes
concepts.prod_example: Linux's struct list_head embeds list pointers directly in kernel objects to avoid separate allocation

concepts.name: Doubly Linked List
concepts.definition: a linked list where each node holds pointers to both the next and previous nodes, enabling bidirectional traversal.
concepts.analogy: a two-lane road where you can drive forward or reverse
concepts.basic_ops: insert anywhere, delete given node reference, traverse forward/backward
concepts.internal_layout: nodes = {value, *prev, *next}; scattered on heap; doubly-linked enables O(1) delete given a node pointer
concepts.memory_note: 16 bytes of pointer overhead per node (two pointers on 64-bit); higher than singly linked
concepts.struct_props: O(1) insert/delete given node pointer, O(n) search, bidirectional traversal
concepts.invariants: head.prev == null; tail.next == null; node.next.prev == node for all interior nodes
concepts.theoretical_note: O(1) deletion given a node pointer is the key advantage over singly linked; used in O(1) cache eviction
concepts.ops_basic:
- Insert at head/tail: O(1)
  - Delete given node: O(1)
  - Traverse: O(n)
  - Search: O(n)
concepts.ops_full:
- Insert before/after given node: O(1)
  - Delete given node: O(1)
  - Search: O(n)
  - Access by index: O(n)
concepts.ops_advanced:
- O(1) delete given node pointer is the defining primitive — enables LRU eviction in O(1)
  - XOR linked list variant halves pointer overhead using XOR of prev and next
  - Sentinel nodes eliminate null checks in hot paths
concepts.space_complexity: O(n) with 2x pointer overhead vs singly linked
concepts.complexity_note: The extra prev pointer enables O(1) delete at the cost of 8 more bytes per node
concepts.amortized_note: All core operations are exactly O(1) with no amortization needed
concepts.beginner_use_case: a browser's back/forward navigation history
concepts.beginner_reason: you need to move forward and backward through pages efficiently
concepts.applications: LRU caches, text editor buffers, deque implementations, OS scheduler run queues
concepts.intermediate_reason: O(1) delete given node + O(1) insert make it perfect for ordered structures that need fast arbitrary removal
concepts.advanced_scenario: implementing an O(1) LRU cache that evicts the least-recently-used item on every access
concepts.advanced_reason: combining a hash map (O(1) lookup) with a doubly linked list (O(1) reorder and evict) achieves O(1) all ops
concepts.real_world: Python OrderedDict (LRU), Linux kernel list_head, Java LinkedHashMap
concepts.advantages:
O(1) insert/delete given node pointer
Bidirectional traversal
Flexible splice and reorder
concepts.disadvantages:
Double pointer overhead
More complex pointer bookkeeping
Poor cache locality
concepts.prefer_this: arbitrary O(1) deletion or backward traversal is needed
concepts.prefer_alt: memory is tight or only forward traversal and head ops are needed
concepts.key_diff: Doubly linked enables O(1) arbitrary delete and reverse traversal; singly linked uses less memory
concepts.limitations: pointer overhead doubles vs singly linked; cache misses are worse due to larger node size
concepts.mitigations: use sentinel/dummy nodes to simplify boundary logic; pool-allocate nodes for better locality
concepts.prod_example: Python's functools.lru_cache uses a doubly linked list + dict to achieve O(1) get and evict

concepts.name: Skip List
concepts.definition: a probabilistic data structure of layered linked lists where each level skips over more elements, enabling O(log n) search on a linked structure.
concepts.analogy: an express train system — local stops at every station, express trains skip to major hubs
concepts.internal_layout: multiple stacked linked lists; each node has up to max_level forward pointers; level assigned randomly with probability p=0.5
concepts.memory_note: O(n log n) expected due to multiple forward pointers per node; ~2 pointers per node on average with p=0.5
concepts.struct_props: O(log n) expected search/insert/delete, O(n) space, probabilistic balance without rotations
concepts.invariants: each higher level is a subset of the level below; level 0 contains all elements; levels are assigned randomly
concepts.theoretical_note: Pugh (1990) proved O(log n) expected time with high probability; max_level = log_{1/p}(n)
concepts.ops_basic:
- Search: O(log n) expected
  - Insert: O(log n) expected
  - Delete: O(log n) expected
concepts.ops_full:
- Search: O(log n) expected, O(n) worst
  - Insert: O(log n) expected
  - Delete: O(log n) expected
  - Range query: O(log n + k)
concepts.ops_advanced:
- Expected O(log n) with high probability: P(cost > c*log n) <= 1/n^(c-1)
  - Lock-free skip list: CAS on forward pointers enables concurrent O(log n) ops
  - Deterministic skip list variant eliminates randomness at the cost of complexity
concepts.space_complexity: O(n) expected with constant ~2 pointers per node (p=0.5)
concepts.complexity_note: Worst-case is O(n) if all nodes end up at level 1, but probability is exponentially small
concepts.amortized_note: No amortization — complexity is probabilistic; expected cost is O(log n) per operation
concepts.beginner_use_case: maintaining a sorted leaderboard where players are frequently added or removed
concepts.beginner_reason: skip list keeps elements sorted and allows fast insert/delete without tree rotations
concepts.applications: Redis sorted sets (ZSET), in-memory databases, concurrent priority queues, range indexes
concepts.intermediate_reason: O(log n) expected ops + simple implementation + cache-friendlier than trees for range queries
concepts.advanced_scenario: a concurrent sorted index where multiple threads insert and delete without locking the whole structure
concepts.advanced_reason: lock-free skip lists use CAS on individual forward pointers, enabling fine-grained concurrency impossible with most trees
concepts.real_world: Redis uses skip list for ZADD/ZRANGE O(log n) sorted set operations
concepts.advantages:
O(log n) expected ops without rotations
Simple implementation vs balanced trees
Natural support for range queries
concepts.disadvantages:
Probabilistic — no worst-case guarantee
Higher memory than BST (multiple pointers per node)
Not cache-friendly
concepts.prefer_this: simplicity of implementation matters or lock-free concurrency is needed
concepts.prefer_alt: worst-case guarantees are required
concepts.key_diff: Skip list is simpler to implement and parallelize; balanced BST gives deterministic O(log n) guarantees
concepts.limitations: no deterministic worst-case; memory overhead scales with max_level; tuning p affects balance
concepts.mitigations: cap max_level at log(n); use p=0.25 to halve average pointers; deterministic variant for real-time systems
concepts.prod_example: Redis ZADD/ZRANGEBYSCORE use a skip list because its range-query and lock-free properties suit in-memory sorted sets

concepts.name: XOR Linked List
concepts.definition: a memory-efficient doubly linked list that stores XOR of previous and next node addresses instead of two separate pointers.
concepts.analogy: a secret passphrase system where you need the current location to decode either neighbor's address
concepts.basic_ops: traverse forward, traverse backward, insert, delete
concepts.internal_layout: each node stores one field: XOR(prev_addr, next_addr); given any node and its predecessor, the successor is computable
concepts.memory_note: one pointer per node instead of two — halves pointer overhead vs doubly linked list
concepts.struct_props: O(1) traversal step given previous node, O(n) full traversal, O(1) insert/delete given neighbors
concepts.invariants: node.both = prev_addr XOR next_addr; head.both = 0 XOR next; tail.both = prev XOR 0
concepts.theoretical_note: Demonstrates that XOR's self-inverse property (A XOR B XOR A = B) enables pointer compression
concepts.ops_basic:
- Traverse step: O(1) given prev pointer
  - Full traversal: O(n)
  - Insert: O(1) given neighbors
concepts.ops_full:
- Traverse: O(n), requires tracking current + previous pointer
  - Insert at position: O(1) given neighbor pointers
  - Delete: O(1) given neighbor pointers
  - Search: O(n)
concepts.ops_advanced:
- All ops same asymptotic as doubly linked list
  - Cannot be used with garbage-collected languages (GC can't trace XOR'd pointers)
  - Not compatible with address sanitizers or debuggers that trace pointer graphs
concepts.space_complexity: O(n) with n pointer slots (half of doubly linked)
concepts.complexity_note: Space saving is exactly 50% of pointer memory; computation adds one XOR per traversal step
concepts.amortized_note: All ops are exactly O(1) per step — no amortization applies
concepts.beginner_use_case: a memory-constrained embedded system needing a doubly-traversable list
concepts.beginner_reason: it achieves bidirectional traversal with the same memory as a singly linked list
concepts.applications: embedded systems, memory-constrained environments, academic OS kernels, low-level systems programming
concepts.intermediate_reason: saves 50% pointer memory vs doubly linked while preserving O(1) bidirectional traversal
concepts.advanced_scenario: an embedded RTOS with 64KB RAM needs a doubly traversable free list for memory allocation
concepts.advanced_reason: XOR linked list fits two-way traversal into singly-linked memory budget — critical when RAM is measured in KB
concepts.real_world: used in academic OS kernels and discussed in Knuth's TAOCP; not common in production due to GC incompatibility
concepts.advantages:
50% less pointer memory than doubly linked
Bidirectional traversal
O(1) insert/delete given neighbors
concepts.disadvantages:
Incompatible with garbage collectors
Harder to debug (pointers not directly readable)
Requires tracking previous node during traversal
concepts.prefer_this: memory is extremely constrained and GC is not used
concepts.prefer_alt: debuggability, GC compatibility, or team familiarity matters
concepts.key_diff: XOR list saves pointer memory; doubly linked list is debuggable and GC-compatible
concepts.limitations: GC incompatibility is a hard blocker in managed languages; debugging requires manual XOR decoding
concepts.mitigations: restrict to unsafe/systems code; add debug-mode wrappers that store plain pointers
concepts.prod_example: Mentioned in Knuth Vol. 1; used in Linux kernel patches (rejected) and niche embedded C projects

concepts.name: Stack
concepts.definition: a last-in, first-out (LIFO) abstract data type where elements are inserted and removed from the same end, called the top.
concepts.analogy: a stack of plates — you always take from the top and add to the top
concepts.basic_ops: push (add to top), pop (remove top), peek (read top)
concepts.internal_layout: typically backed by a dynamic array (top = last index) or linked list (top = head)
concepts.memory_note: array-backed: contiguous; linked-backed: scattered; array-backed is preferred for cache performance
concepts.struct_props: O(1) push/pop/peek, LIFO order, no random access
concepts.invariants: only the top element is accessible; push and pop maintain LIFO invariant
concepts.theoretical_note: Stacks are the computational model behind recursion — call stacks implement function invocation as a stack
concepts.ops_basic:
- Push: O(1)
  - Pop: O(1)
  - Peek: O(1)
  - Search: O(n)
concepts.ops_full:
- Push: O(1) amortized (array-backed)
  - Pop: O(1)
  - Peek: O(1)
  - Size: O(1)
  - Search: O(n)
concepts.ops_advanced:
- Array-backed push: O(1) amortized by doubling
  - Min-stack augmentation: O(1) min query by maintaining parallel min stack
  - Persistent stack: O(1) push/pop with O(n) total space via structural sharing
concepts.complexity_note: A min-stack doubles space but adds O(1) minimum query — classic space-time tradeoff
concepts.amortized_note: Array-backed push is O(1) amortized; each resize costs O(n) but happens at most once per n pushes
concepts.beginner_use_case: checking whether parentheses in a math expression are balanced
concepts.beginner_reason: each opening bracket is pushed; each closing bracket pops and verifies the match
concepts.applications: function call stacks, undo/redo, expression evaluation, DFS traversal, backtracking algorithms
concepts.intermediate_reason: LIFO order naturally models recursive and backtracking problems without actual recursion
concepts.advanced_scenario: converting a recursive DFS to iterative to avoid stack overflow on deep graphs
concepts.advanced_reason: explicit stack mirrors the call stack but lives on the heap — no OS stack size limit
concepts.real_world: CPU call stack, Python sys.setrecursionlimit workaround via explicit stack, browser history
concepts.advantages:
O(1) push/pop/peek
Simple LIFO semantics
Natural fit for recursive algorithms
concepts.disadvantages:
No random access
Only top element visible
O(n) search
concepts.prefer_this: LIFO order is needed (DFS, undo, call tracking)
concepts.prefer_alt: FIFO order is needed (BFS, scheduling, buffering)
concepts.key_diff: Stack is LIFO; Queue is FIFO — the access order is the defining difference
concepts.limitations: fixed stack depth on CPU stack; no random access; not suitable for FIFO workloads
concepts.mitigations: use heap-allocated explicit stack for deep recursion; augment with min/max tracking for O(1) queries
concepts.prod_example: Python's ast module uses an explicit stack to evaluate expression trees iteratively

concepts.name: Queue
concepts.definition: a first-in, first-out (FIFO) abstract data type where elements are added at the rear and removed from the front.
concepts.analogy: a line at a ticket counter — first person in line is served first
concepts.basic_ops: enqueue (add to rear), dequeue (remove from front), peek front
concepts.internal_layout: typically backed by a circular buffer (O(1) enqueue/dequeue) or doubly linked list
concepts.memory_note: circular buffer: fixed contiguous allocation; linked: dynamic per-node allocation
concepts.struct_props: O(1) enqueue/dequeue, FIFO order, no random access
concepts.invariants: elements exit in the same order they enter; front and rear maintained as separate pointers
concepts.theoretical_note: FIFO queues are the scheduling primitive behind BFS and most OS process schedulers
concepts.ops_basic:
- Enqueue: O(1)
  - Dequeue: O(1)
  - Peek: O(1)
  - Search: O(n)
concepts.ops_full:
- Enqueue: O(1) amortized
  - Dequeue: O(1)
  - Front/rear peek: O(1)
  - Size: O(1)
  - Search: O(n)
concepts.ops_advanced:
- Lock-free queue (Michael and Scott 1996): CAS on tail for concurrent enqueue, CAS on head for dequeue
  - Work-stealing deque: each thread has a FIFO front (steal) + LIFO rear (local push/pop)
  - Persistent queue: O(1) amortized via lazy evaluation (Okasaki)
concepts.complexity_note: Array-backed queue requires circular indexing to avoid O(n) shifts on dequeue
concepts.amortized_note: Two-stack queue achieves O(1) amortized enqueue/dequeue: enqueue to stack1, dequeue from stack2 (refill lazily)
concepts.beginner_use_case: printing documents — first document submitted is printed first
concepts.beginner_reason: FIFO ensures fairness — the order of submission is preserved
concepts.applications: BFS traversal, OS process scheduling, network packet buffering, task queues, event loops
concepts.intermediate_reason: FIFO order guarantees level-by-level processing in BFS and fair scheduling in task systems
concepts.advanced_scenario: a multi-threaded task executor needs a lock-free work queue shared across 32 worker threads
concepts.advanced_reason: Michael-Scott lock-free queue uses CAS operations to allow concurrent enqueue and dequeue without mutex contention
concepts.real_world: Java ConcurrentLinkedQueue, Linux task_struct run queue, Node.js event loop microtask queue
concepts.advantages:
O(1) enqueue and dequeue
Fair FIFO ordering
Simple semantics
concepts.disadvantages:
No random access
Fixed-size if circular buffer backed
No priority ordering
concepts.prefer_this: strict FIFO ordering is required (BFS, scheduling)
concepts.prefer_alt: elements need to be processed by priority rather than arrival order
concepts.key_diff: Queue is FIFO and O(1) all ops; Priority Queue is ordered by priority but O(log n) insert/remove
concepts.limitations: no priority; fixed-size circular buffer requires overflow handling; head-of-line blocking in single queues
concepts.mitigations: use priority queue for prioritized workloads; use multiple queues (multi-level feedback queue) for scheduling
concepts.prod_example: Linux CFS scheduler uses a red-black tree as a priority queue, not a plain FIFO queue

concepts.name: Deque
concepts.definition: a double-ended queue that supports O(1) insertion and deletion from both the front and the rear.
concepts.analogy: a subway car with doors at both ends — passengers can board or exit from either side
concepts.basic_ops: push_front, push_back, pop_front, pop_back, peek_front, peek_back
concepts.internal_layout: typically as a ring buffer or a doubly linked list; Python uses a doubly-linked list of fixed-size blocks
concepts.memory_note: ring-buffer backed: contiguous; linked backed: scattered with pointer overhead
concepts.struct_props: O(1) all four push/pop operations, O(1) peek both ends, O(n) indexed access
concepts.invariants: both ends are directly accessible; front and back pointers maintained; FIFO and LIFO both achievable
concepts.theoretical_note: Deque generalizes both Stack and Queue — any algorithm using either can use a Deque without loss
concepts.ops_basic:
- push_front / push_back: O(1)
  - pop_front / pop_back: O(1)
  - Peek both ends: O(1)
concepts.ops_full:
- All four push/pop: O(1) amortized
  - Peek front/back: O(1)
  - Access by index: O(1) for array-backed, O(n) for linked
  - Search: O(n)
concepts.ops_advanced:
- Work-stealing: workers push/pop from back (LIFO); idle workers steal from front (FIFO)
  - Sliding window max/min: deque of indices in O(n) total
  - C++ std::deque: segmented array gives O(1) push_front without full copy
concepts.complexity_note: std::deque uses a map of fixed-size chunks — push_front allocates a new chunk only at boundaries
concepts.amortized_note: Array-backed deque uses circular indexing with doubling; all ops are O(1) amortized
concepts.beginner_use_case: a playlist that supports adding songs to both the front and the end
concepts.beginner_reason: deque allows inserting priority songs at the front without rebuilding the list
concepts.applications: sliding window algorithms, work-stealing schedulers, palindrome checking, browser tab history
concepts.intermediate_reason: O(1) both-end ops make it the right choice for any algorithm needing a sliding window or double-sided buffer
concepts.advanced_scenario: implementing a parallel work-stealing thread pool (e.g., Java ForkJoinPool)
concepts.advanced_reason: each thread maintains a deque: LIFO from the back (cache-warm), FIFO steal from the front (load balance)
concepts.real_world: Java ForkJoinPool worker deques, Python collections.deque, C++ std::deque
concepts.advantages:
O(1) push/pop from both ends
Generalizes Stack and Queue
Efficient sliding window support
concepts.disadvantages:
More complex implementation than stack or queue
O(n) middle access for linked variant
Higher memory overhead
concepts.prefer_this: efficient access at both ends is needed
concepts.prefer_alt: only one-end access is needed and cache performance is critical
concepts.key_diff: Deque adds O(1) front ops that plain arrays lack; array wins on random access and cache density
concepts.limitations: indexed access is O(n) for linked variant; middle insertion is O(n); not a drop-in for arrays
concepts.mitigations: use circular buffer variant for O(1) indexed access; Python deque maxlen auto-discards old items
concepts.prod_example: Python collections.deque with maxlen implements a fixed sliding window used in moving-average calculations

concepts.name: Priority Queue
concepts.definition: an abstract data type where each element has a priority, and the element with the highest (or lowest) priority is always served first.
concepts.analogy: an emergency room triage system — the most critical patient is treated next, not whoever arrived first
concepts.internal_layout: almost always implemented as a binary heap (array-backed); alternatives include Fibonacci heap, Pairing heap
concepts.memory_note: binary heap: O(n) contiguous array; Fibonacci heap: O(n) pointer-linked forest
concepts.struct_props: O(log n) insert and extract, O(1) peek; binary heap is cache-friendly
concepts.invariants: the root is always the min (or max); heap property maintained after every insert/extract
concepts.theoretical_note: Dijkstra's algorithm runs in O((V+E) log V) with a binary heap PQ; O(V log V + E) with Fibonacci heap
concepts.ops_basic:
- Insert: O(log n)
  - Extract min/max: O(log n)
  - Peek min/max: O(1)
concepts.ops_full:
- Insert: O(log n)
  - Extract min: O(log n)
  - Peek min: O(1)
  - Decrease key: O(log n) binary heap, O(1) amortized Fibonacci heap
  - Build from array: O(n)
concepts.ops_advanced:
- Binary heap build: O(n) via Floyd's sift-down (not O(n log n) insert loop)
  - Fibonacci heap decrease_key: O(1) amortized — key for Dijkstra on dense graphs
  - Brodal queue: O(1) worst-case insert and decrease_key, O(log n) extract
concepts.complexity_note: Build-heap is O(n) not O(n log n) — most elements are near leaves and sift-down cost is small
concepts.amortized_note: Fibonacci heap achieves O(1) amortized insert and decrease_key via lazy consolidation
concepts.beginner_use_case: finding the top-3 highest scores in a game leaderboard
concepts.beginner_reason: a max-heap always keeps the highest score at the root for O(1) access
concepts.applications: Dijkstra's algorithm, A* pathfinding, Huffman encoding, task schedulers, event-driven simulation
concepts.intermediate_reason: O(log n) ordered insert/extract is the sweet spot for algorithms that repeatedly need the next best element
concepts.advanced_scenario: implementing Dijkstra on a graph with 10M nodes and 100M edges where decrease_key is called billions of times
concepts.advanced_reason: Fibonacci heap reduces decrease_key from O(log n) to O(1) amortized, reducing total complexity from O(E log V) to O(E + V log V)
concepts.real_world: Python heapq, Java PriorityQueue, C++ std::priority_queue, Dijkstra in routing protocols (OSPF)
concepts.advantages:
O(log n) ordered insert and extract
O(1) peek at min/max
O(n) build from unsorted array
concepts.disadvantages:
O(n) search for arbitrary element
No efficient arbitrary delete (without augmentation)
Fibonacci heap complex to implement
concepts.prefer_this: frequent insertions mixed with extractions are needed
concepts.prefer_alt: data is static and only extraction is needed (sorted array suffices)
concepts.key_diff: Priority queue handles mixed insert/extract in O(log n); sorted array gives O(1) extract but O(n) insert
concepts.limitations: O(n) search; no O(1) arbitrary delete without index augmentation; Fibonacci heap high constant factor
concepts.mitigations: use lazy deletion (mark deleted elements, skip on extract); augment with hash map for O(log n) decrease_key
concepts.prod_example: NetworkX Dijkstra uses heapq with lazy deletion to handle decrease_key without modifying the heap

concepts.name: Binary Search Tree
concepts.definition: a binary tree where each node's left subtree contains only keys less than the node's key, and the right subtree contains only keys greater.
concepts.analogy: a filing cabinet where files smaller than the label go left and larger ones go right at every drawer
concepts.internal_layout: linked nodes {key, value, *left, *right, *parent}; structure depends on insertion order
concepts.memory_note: O(n) nodes with 3-4 pointers each; no contiguous memory; height determines performance
concepts.struct_props: O(h) search/insert/delete where h = height; in-order yields sorted sequence
concepts.invariants: left subtree keys < root key < right subtree keys; this holds recursively for every node
concepts.theoretical_note: BST height is O(log n) expected for random insertion but O(n) worst case; balanced variants enforce O(log n)
concepts.ops_basic:
- Search: O(log n) avg, O(n) worst
  - Insert: O(log n) avg, O(n) worst
  - Delete: O(log n) avg, O(n) worst
concepts.ops_full:
- Search/insert/delete: O(h)
  - Min/max: O(h)
  - Successor/predecessor: O(h)
  - In-order traversal: O(n)
concepts.ops_advanced:
- Expected height for n random inserts: ~1.386 log n
  - Delete with successor replacement maintains BST property
  - Treap and randomized BST probabilistically guarantee O(log n) height
concepts.complexity_note: Degenerate BST (sorted insertion) becomes a linked list with O(n) ops — motivates AVL and Red-Black trees
concepts.amortized_note: No amortization — all ops depend on current height; use balanced variants for guarantees
concepts.beginner_use_case: storing a dictionary of words and looking them up alphabetically
concepts.beginner_reason: BST search halves the search space at each step, like binary search but for dynamic data
concepts.applications: database indexes, symbol tables, sorted set implementations, range queries
concepts.intermediate_reason: O(log n) expected ops + in-order sorted traversal make it foundational for ordered dictionaries
concepts.advanced_scenario: need a dynamic sorted set that supports predecessor, successor, and rank queries efficiently
concepts.advanced_reason: augmented BST (order-statistics tree) adds subtree size to each node, enabling O(log n) rank and select
concepts.real_world: SQLite BTree index, STL std::map (Red-Black BST), Python sortedcontainers SortedList
concepts.advantages:
O(log n) average search/insert/delete
In-order traversal gives sorted output
Supports range queries natively
concepts.disadvantages:
O(n) worst case if unbalanced
Pointer overhead per node
Complex delete (three cases)
concepts.prefer_this: sorted order, range queries, or predecessor/successor queries are needed
concepts.prefer_alt: only exact-match lookup is needed and order doesn't matter
concepts.key_diff: BST maintains sorted order for O(log n) range queries; hash table gives O(1) lookup but no order
concepts.limitations: worst-case O(n) without balancing; complex to implement correctly with all edge cases
concepts.mitigations: use AVL or Red-Black tree for guaranteed O(log n); use treap for simpler probabilistic balance
concepts.prod_example: PostgreSQL uses B-tree indexes (generalized BST) for all indexed column lookups

concepts.name: AVL Tree
concepts.definition: a self-balancing BST where the heights of the left and right subtrees of every node differ by at most 1, maintained via rotations.
concepts.analogy: a BST that automatically re-levels itself like a balanced scale after every insertion or deletion
concepts.internal_layout: nodes = {key, value, *left, *right, height}; balance factor = height(left) - height(right) in {-1,0,1}
concepts.memory_note: one extra integer (height or balance factor) per node beyond a plain BST node
concepts.struct_props: guaranteed O(log n) for all ops; strictest height balance of standard BSTs
concepts.invariants: balance factor of every node is in {-1, 0, 1}; height updates propagate up on insert/delete
concepts.theoretical_note: AVL trees have height <= 1.44 log(n+2) — tighter than Red-Black trees (<= 2 log(n+1))
concepts.ops_basic:
- Search: O(log n)
  - Insert: O(log n)
  - Delete: O(log n)
concepts.ops_full:
- Search/insert/delete: O(log n) guaranteed
  - Rotation on insert: at most 2 rotations
  - Rotation on delete: O(log n) rotations
  - In-order traversal: O(n)
concepts.ops_advanced:
- Maximum rotations on insert: 2 (one LL/RR or LR/RL)
  - Delete may trigger O(log n) rotations up the path
  - Strictly balanced height: lookup-heavy workloads favor AVL over Red-Black
concepts.complexity_note: AVL has lower height than Red-Black -> faster lookups; but more rotations on insert -> slower writes
concepts.amortized_note: All ops are worst-case O(log n); no amortization needed
concepts.beginner_use_case: an autocomplete dictionary that is frequently searched but rarely updated
concepts.beginner_reason: AVL's strict balance gives faster lookups than Red-Black, ideal for read-heavy workloads
concepts.applications: in-memory databases, sorted sets in read-heavy systems, geometric data structures
concepts.intermediate_reason: tighter height bound than Red-Black makes AVL better for lookup-heavy workloads at the cost of more rebalancing
concepts.advanced_scenario: a real-time system needs the minimum possible lookup latency for a sorted set with infrequent updates
concepts.advanced_reason: AVL's height <= 1.44 log n reduces expected comparisons per lookup vs Red-Black's 2 log n bound
concepts.real_world: Used in some database engines and the Linux ext2 filesystem directory lookup tables
concepts.advantages:
Guaranteed O(log n) all ops
Stricter balance than Red-Black -> faster lookups
Well-studied and predictable
concepts.disadvantages:
More rotations on insert/delete than Red-Black
Extra height field per node
Complex implementation
concepts.prefer_this: read-heavy workloads where lookup speed is critical
concepts.prefer_alt: write-heavy workloads where fewer rotations matter more than lookup speed
concepts.key_diff: AVL is more strictly balanced (faster lookup); Red-Black does fewer rotations (faster write)
concepts.limitations: more rotations on delete than Red-Black; height field adds memory per node
concepts.mitigations: use Red-Black for write-heavy; use B-tree for disk-based storage where fan-out matters more than depth
concepts.prod_example: Some Java TreeMap benchmarks show AVL outperforming Red-Black for read-dominated workloads

concepts.name: Red-Black Tree
concepts.definition: a self-balancing BST where nodes are colored red or black and five invariants ensure the tree height stays within 2 log(n+1).
concepts.analogy: a BST with a color-coded balancing rulebook that trades perfect balance for fewer rebalancing operations
concepts.internal_layout: nodes = {key, value, color, *left, *right, *parent}; color is 1 bit but usually stored as a byte
concepts.memory_note: one extra color bit per node; in practice, stored as a byte or in a pointer's low bit
concepts.struct_props: guaranteed O(log n) all ops; at most 2 rotations on insert, O(1) rotations amortized on delete
concepts.invariants: root is black; red nodes have black children; every path root to leaf has same black-node count
concepts.theoretical_note: Red-Black trees are equivalent to 2-3-4 trees; height <= 2 log(n+1) guaranteed
concepts.ops_basic:
- Search: O(log n)
  - Insert: O(log n)
  - Delete: O(log n)
concepts.ops_full:
- All ops: O(log n) worst case
  - Insert: at most 2 rotations + O(log n) recolors
  - Delete: at most 3 rotations + O(log n) recolors
concepts.ops_advanced:
- Amortized O(1) structural changes per insert/delete (Tarjan 1985)
  - Functional (persistent) Red-Black tree: O(log n) insert with path copying
  - Used in interval trees and order-statistics trees via node augmentation
concepts.complexity_note: Height <= 2 log(n+1); in practice closer to 1.7 log n for random data
concepts.amortized_note: Tarjan proved O(1) amortized rotations per operation — making Red-Black ideal for write-heavy workloads
concepts.beginner_use_case: a dynamic set of unique usernames that needs fast insert, delete, and search
concepts.beginner_reason: Red-Black tree guarantees O(log n) for all three operations regardless of insertion order
concepts.applications: Java TreeMap/TreeSet, C++ std::map/std::set, Linux CFS scheduler, nginx timer tree
concepts.intermediate_reason: O(1) amortized rotations and O(log n) worst-case all ops make it the standard ordered map implementation
concepts.advanced_scenario: a kernel scheduler needs to insert/remove tasks with O(log n) guarantee and minimal structural changes
concepts.advanced_reason: Red-Black's O(1) amortized rotations keep cache invalidation minimal in hot scheduler paths
concepts.real_world: Linux CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler) uses a Red-Black tree keyed by virtual runtime
concepts.advantages:
O(log n) guaranteed all ops
Fewer rotations than AVL on writes
Widely implemented and battle-tested
concepts.disadvantages:
Complex invariant maintenance (5 rules)
Slightly higher height than AVL -> slightly slower lookup
Color adds memory
concepts.prefer_this: write-heavy workloads or when amortized structural changes matter
concepts.prefer_alt: read-heavy workloads where tighter height bound improves lookup speed
concepts.key_diff: Red-Black does fewer rotations (better for writes); AVL has tighter height bound (better for reads)
concepts.limitations: 5 invariants make correct implementation error-prone; height slightly worse than AVL
concepts.mitigations: use a well-tested library implementation; verify with property-based testing
concepts.prod_example: Linux kernel rbtree.h is used in CFS scheduler, memory manager, and epoll

concepts.name: B-Tree
concepts.definition: a self-balancing search tree where nodes can have multiple keys and children, designed to minimize disk I/O by keeping the tree shallow and nodes large.
concepts.analogy: a library catalog with wide drawers — each drawer holds many index cards, reducing the number of drawers you need to open
concepts.internal_layout: nodes hold up to 2t-1 keys and 2t children; all leaves at the same depth; order t chosen to match disk page size
concepts.memory_note: node size chosen to match OS page (4KB-16KB); one node read = one disk I/O
concepts.struct_props: height = O(log_t n); all ops = O(log n) disk I/Os; nodes always between half-full and full
concepts.invariants: all leaves same depth; every non-root node has >= t-1 keys; root has >= 1 key; nodes have <= 2t-1 keys
concepts.theoretical_note: B-tree height = ceil(log_t((n+1)/2)); large t (e.g., t=100) gives height 2-3 for billions of records
concepts.ops_basic:
- Search: O(log n) disk I/Os
  - Insert: O(log n) disk I/Os
  - Delete: O(log n) disk I/Os
concepts.ops_full:
- Search: O(t log_t n) key comparisons, O(log_t n) disk reads
  - Insert: O(log_t n) — splits propagate up
  - Delete: O(log_t n) — merges/redistributions propagate up
concepts.ops_advanced:
- B+ tree variant: all data in leaves + leaf linked list enables O(log n + k) range scan
  - Copy-on-write B-tree: used in BTRFS/ZFS for atomic updates
  - Fractal tree: buffers writes in nodes for O(log^2 n / B) I/Os amortized
concepts.complexity_note: Large t drastically reduces height — t=1000 gives height 2 for 1 billion records
concepts.amortized_note: Each split/merge propagates at most to root; total work per insert/delete is O(log_t n) node reads/writes
concepts.beginner_use_case: a database storing millions of customer records indexed by ID
concepts.beginner_reason: B-tree keeps the index shallow so finding any record requires only 2-3 disk reads
concepts.applications: database indexes (PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB), filesystems (NTFS, ext4, HFS+, BTRFS)
concepts.intermediate_reason: designed to match disk page sizes — each node fits in one I/O, minimizing the most expensive operation
concepts.advanced_scenario: designing a storage engine for a database with 1TB of data on spinning disks
concepts.advanced_reason: B+ tree with page-sized nodes reduces I/O to O(log_t n) ~= 3-4 for any record, making disk seek the bottleneck not algorithm depth
concepts.real_world: MySQL InnoDB uses B+ trees for all indexes; PostgreSQL heap files use B-tree indexes for primary keys
concepts.advantages:
Minimizes disk I/Os
Self-balancing
Excellent for range queries (B+ variant)
concepts.disadvantages:
Complex split/merge logic
Overkill for in-memory use (binary heap or BST preferable)
High implementation complexity
concepts.prefer_this: data is on disk and I/O minimization is critical
concepts.prefer_alt: data is in memory and a simpler structure suffices
concepts.key_diff: B-tree is optimized for disk I/O with high branching factor; BST is simpler but deep on disk
concepts.limitations: complex split/merge; node size tuning is critical; CoW B-trees add write amplification
concepts.mitigations: use B+ tree for range-scan workloads; tune page size to match storage block size
concepts.prod_example: InnoDB's clustered B+ tree index stores full row data in leaf nodes for O(log n) primary-key lookups

concepts.name: Binary Heap
concepts.definition: a complete binary tree stored as an array where every parent node is smaller (min-heap) or larger (max-heap) than its children.
concepts.analogy: a tournament bracket where the winner (min/max) is always at the top
concepts.internal_layout: packed array: root at index 1; for node i, left child = 2i, right child = 2i+1, parent = i//2
concepts.memory_note: single contiguous array — no pointers; cache-friendly; child index computed arithmetically
concepts.struct_props: O(log n) insert/extract, O(1) peek, O(n) build; complete tree guarantees height = floor(log n)
concepts.invariants: heap property: A[i] <= A[2i] and A[i] <= A[2i+1] for all i (min-heap); tree is complete
concepts.theoretical_note: Floyd's build-heap is O(n) not O(n log n): most nodes are near leaves with small sift-down cost
concepts.ops_basic:
- Insert: O(log n)
  - Extract min: O(log n)
  - Peek min: O(1)
concepts.ops_full:
- Insert: O(log n)
  - Extract min/max: O(log n)
  - Peek: O(1)
  - Build from array: O(n)
  - Decrease key: O(log n)
concepts.ops_advanced:
- Floyd's heapify: O(n) because sum of sift-down costs converges to O(n)
  - Heapsort: O(n log n) worst-case in-place sort
  - d-ary heap: reduces height to log_d(n), trading more comparisons per level for fewer levels
concepts.complexity_note: Build-heap O(n) not O(n log n) — mathematical derivation shows sum of sift-down costs converges to O(n)
concepts.amortized_note: No amortization needed — sift-up/down height is exactly floor(log n)
concepts.beginner_use_case: always finding the cheapest flight in a list of options
concepts.beginner_reason: min-heap keeps the cheapest option at the top for O(1) access, adding new flights in O(log n)
concepts.applications: Dijkstra's algorithm, Prim's MST, heap sort, OS timer queues, median-of-stream (two heaps)
concepts.intermediate_reason: O(n) build + O(log n) extract makes it the standard priority queue backend
concepts.advanced_scenario: finding the running median of a stream of integers with O(log n) per update
concepts.advanced_reason: two heaps (max-heap for lower half, min-heap for upper half) maintain median at the top of each
concepts.real_world: Python heapq module, Java PriorityQueue, C++ std::priority_queue
concepts.advantages:
O(n) build from array
Cache-friendly array storage (no pointers)
Simple index arithmetic
concepts.disadvantages:
O(n) arbitrary search
No O(1) decrease_key without index augmentation
Not sorted for arbitrary access
concepts.prefer_this: simplicity and cache performance are important
concepts.prefer_alt: O(1) amortized decrease_key is needed for graph algorithms on dense graphs
concepts.key_diff: Binary heap is simple and cache-friendly; Fibonacci heap adds O(1) decrease_key at the cost of complexity
concepts.limitations: O(n) search; O(log n) decrease_key requires knowing element index; not stable
concepts.mitigations: augment with hash map for index tracking; use lazy deletion for decrease_key workaround
concepts.prod_example: Python's heapq is used in the standard library's heapq.nsmallest and in asyncio's event loop timer

concepts.name: Fibonacci Heap
concepts.definition: a lazy heap structure consisting of a forest of heap-ordered trees, achieving O(1) amortized insert and decrease_key by deferring consolidation.
concepts.analogy: a messy desk that defers organizing until absolutely necessary — fast to add things, organized only when you must retrieve the minimum
concepts.internal_layout: a doubly-linked circular list of heap-ordered trees; root list pointers; min pointer maintained separately
concepts.memory_note: O(n) nodes with ~6 pointers each (parent, child, left, right, degree, mark); high constant factor
concepts.struct_props: O(1) amortized insert/decrease_key, O(log n) amortized extract_min, O(1) merge
concepts.invariants: each tree satisfies heap order; degree-d tree has >= F(d+2) nodes (Fibonacci bound); mark bit tracks second child loss
concepts.theoretical_note: Fibonacci heap achieves the theoretically optimal O(E + V log V) Dijkstra complexity on dense graphs
concepts.ops_basic:
- Insert: O(1) amortized
  - Find min: O(1)
  - Extract min: O(log n) amortized
concepts.ops_full:
- Insert: O(1) amortized
  - Find min: O(1)
  - Extract min: O(log n) amortized
  - Decrease key: O(1) amortized
  - Merge two heaps: O(1)
concepts.ops_advanced:
- Potential function Phi = trees + 2*marked_nodes
  - Extract_min consolidates root list: O(log n) amortized
  - Cascading cut: when a node loses a second child, it is cut to root list (maintains Fibonacci degree bound)
concepts.space_complexity: O(n) with large pointer constant (~6 per node)
concepts.complexity_note: High constant factor makes Fibonacci heap slower than binary heap in practice for most n encountered
concepts.amortized_note: Potential method: Phi = #trees + 2*#marked; each op's amortized cost = actual cost + delta_Phi proves O(1) for insert/decrease_key
concepts.beginner_use_case: a theoretical algorithm class example of why Dijkstra runs faster on dense graphs
concepts.beginner_reason: Fibonacci heap's O(1) decrease_key reduces Dijkstra from O(E log V) to O(E + V log V)
concepts.applications: theoretical graph algorithms (Dijkstra, Prim on dense graphs), network flow, research
concepts.intermediate_reason: the only standard heap with O(1) amortized decrease_key — critical for graph algorithms with many relaxations
concepts.advanced_scenario: running Dijkstra on a complete graph (E = V^2) where decrease_key dominates cost
concepts.advanced_reason: binary heap Dijkstra: O(V^2 log V); Fibonacci heap: O(V^2) — the decrease_key savings are asymptotically significant
concepts.real_world: Used in academic implementations of Dijkstra; rarely in production due to high constant factors
concepts.advantages:
O(1) amortized insert and decrease_key
O(1) heap merge
Optimal theoretical complexity for graph algorithms
concepts.disadvantages:
Very high constant factor (complex pointer manipulation)
Poor cache performance
Rarely faster than binary heap in practice
concepts.prefer_this: theoretical optimality matters or dense-graph algorithm complexity must be minimized
concepts.prefer_alt: practical performance is needed — binary heap almost always wins in benchmarks
concepts.key_diff: Fibonacci heap is theoretically optimal; binary heap is practically faster due to cache and simplicity
concepts.limitations: complex cascading cut logic; poor cache behavior; high pointer overhead; rarely practical
concepts.mitigations: use Pairing heap as a practical alternative with similar amortized bounds and simpler implementation
concepts.prod_example: NetworkX falls back to binary heap for Dijkstra because Fibonacci heap's overhead outweighs its asymptotic gains at typical scale

concepts.name: Min-Max Heap
concepts.definition: a double-ended priority queue stored as a heap where both the minimum and maximum can be found and extracted in O(log n).
concepts.analogy: a two-sided scoreboard that always shows both the lowest and highest scores simultaneously
concepts.internal_layout: array with alternating min and max levels; level 0 (root) is a min level; level 1 is a max level; alternates down
concepts.memory_note: same as binary heap — single array, no extra pointers; only the comparison direction changes by level
concepts.struct_props: O(log n) all ops, O(1) find_min and find_max, single array storage
concepts.invariants: on min levels, node <= all descendants; on max levels, node >= all descendants; levels alternate min/max
concepts.theoretical_note: Atkinson et al. (1986) proved min-max heap achieves optimal O(log n) for both extract_min and extract_max
concepts.ops_basic:
- Find min: O(1)
  - Find max: O(1)
  - Insert: O(log n)
  - Extract min or max: O(log n)
concepts.ops_full:
- Find min/max: O(1)
  - Insert: O(log n)
  - Extract min: O(log n)
  - Extract max: O(log n)
  - Build: O(n)
concepts.ops_advanced:
- Insert: bubble-up alternates between min and max comparisons by level
  - Extract_min: replace with last element, trickle-down on min levels
  - Interval heap variant: each node stores [min, max] pair for potentially better cache behavior
concepts.space_complexity: O(n) — same array layout as binary heap
concepts.complexity_note: Only constant factor overhead vs binary heap — same array, same index arithmetic, just alternating comparison
concepts.amortized_note: All ops are worst-case O(log n) — no amortization needed
concepts.beginner_use_case: a double-sided job queue that needs both the highest-priority and lowest-priority jobs efficiently
concepts.beginner_reason: min-max heap gives O(1) access to both extremes without maintaining two separate heaps
concepts.applications: external sorting (double-ended queues), sliding window min/max, A* with bidirectional bounds, memory allocators
concepts.intermediate_reason: replaces two heaps (one min, one max) with a single structure using the same memory
concepts.advanced_scenario: an external sort algorithm needs to simultaneously access both the smallest and largest current keys
concepts.advanced_reason: min-max heap provides O(log n) extract at both ends in one structure, halving memory vs two-heap approach
concepts.real_world: Used in external sort implementations and some database query executors needing range-bounded priority queues
concepts.advantages:
O(1) find_min and find_max
Single array (cache-friendly)
Halves memory vs two-heap approach
concepts.disadvantages:
More complex insert/extract logic than standard heap
Less well-known than binary heap
Slightly higher constant factor
concepts.prefer_this: both min and max access are needed with minimal memory
concepts.prefer_alt: only one end (min or max) needs to be accessed
concepts.key_diff: Min-max heap handles both ends in one structure; two heaps are simpler but use 2x the memory
concepts.limitations: more complex trickle-down logic with level-aware comparisons; less battle-tested than binary heap
concepts.mitigations: test thoroughly with property-based testing; consider interval heap variant for potentially better cache behavior
concepts.prod_example: Some external merge-sort implementations use min-max heaps to bound both the minimum and maximum active records

concepts.name: Leftist Heap
concepts.definition: a heap-ordered binary tree where every node's left subtree has rank (rightmost path length) >= right subtree rank, enabling O(log n) merging.
concepts.analogy: a lopsided tree that always keeps its weight on the left, making it easy to attach new branches on the right
concepts.basic_ops: merge, insert (as merge with singleton), extract_min (as merge of left and right children)
concepts.internal_layout: nodes = {key, *left, *right, rank}; rank = length of rightmost path to null; skewed left by construction
concepts.memory_note: O(n) nodes with rank field; pointer-based — no array backing; less cache-friendly than binary heap
concepts.struct_props: O(log n) merge/insert/extract; right spine has length <= log(n+1); all ops reduce to merge
concepts.invariants: heap property holds; rank(left) >= rank(right) for every node; rank = 1 + min(rank(left), rank(right))
concepts.theoretical_note: Right spine length <= log(n+1) because merging always works on the right spine — key to O(log n) merge
concepts.ops_basic:
- Merge: O(log n)
  - Insert: O(log n)
  - Extract min: O(log n)
concepts.ops_full:
- Merge two heaps: O(log n)
  - Insert: O(log n) (merge with singleton)
  - Extract min: O(log n) (merge left and right subtrees)
  - Find min: O(1)
concepts.ops_advanced:
- All ops reduce to merge — simplifies correctness proofs
  - Skew heap: removes rank field, uses randomized swapping; O(log n) amortized merge
  - Leftist heap is deterministic; skew heap trades determinism for simplicity
concepts.space_complexity: O(n) with rank field per node
concepts.complexity_note: Merge is the primitive — insert and extract are just special merges. Right spine <= log n by rank invariant.
concepts.amortized_note: Deterministic O(log n) worst-case merge; no amortization needed (unlike skew heap)
concepts.beginner_use_case: merging two priority queues from two sorted task lists into one
concepts.beginner_reason: leftist heap merges two heaps in O(log n) — far better than rebuilding from scratch
concepts.applications: mergeable heap applications, event simulation, discrete-event simulators, Prim's MST with repeated merges
concepts.intermediate_reason: O(log n) merge makes it the right choice when priority queues themselves need to be combined frequently
concepts.advanced_scenario: a parallel simulation where each worker has its own event queue and workers need to merge queues periodically
concepts.advanced_reason: O(log n) merge allows efficient queue combination without O(n) rebuilding
concepts.real_world: Used in discrete-event simulation frameworks and some functional programming implementations of mergeable heaps
concepts.advantages:
O(log n) merge — unique among simple heaps
All ops reduce to merge (elegant design)
Deterministic O(log n)
concepts.disadvantages:
Pointer-based — worse cache performance than binary heap
Extra rank field per node
Not as widely known or tested
concepts.prefer_this: heap merging is a frequent operation
concepts.prefer_alt: merge is rare and cache performance or simplicity matters more
concepts.key_diff: Leftist heap enables O(log n) merge; binary heap cannot merge two heaps faster than O(n)
concepts.limitations: pointer-based hurts cache performance; rank adds memory; not suitable as a drop-in for binary heap in typical use
concepts.mitigations: use skew heap if rank field overhead is problematic; use Fibonacci heap if decrease_key also matters
concepts.prod_example: Some event-driven simulation engines use leftist or skew heaps to merge event queues from spawned sub-simulations

concepts.name: Hash Table
concepts.definition: a data structure that maps keys to values using a hash function to compute an array index, enabling O(1) average-case lookup, insert, and delete.
concepts.analogy: a library where the librarian immediately computes which shelf any book lives on from its title, without searching
concepts.internal_layout: an array of buckets; each bucket holds either a value (open addressing) or a linked list of entries (chaining)
concepts.memory_note: O(n) entries but typically O(n/load_factor) array slots; load factor kept below 0.75 for performance
concepts.struct_props: O(1) average insert/lookup/delete; O(n) worst case with hash collisions
concepts.invariants: load factor = n / capacity; resize (rehash) triggered when load factor exceeds threshold
concepts.theoretical_note: With universal hashing and chaining, expected chain length = load factor alpha; O(1+alpha) expected per op
concepts.ops_basic:
- Lookup: O(1) average
  - Insert: O(1) average
  - Delete: O(1) average
concepts.ops_full:
- Lookup: O(1) avg, O(n) worst
  - Insert: O(1) amortized
  - Delete: O(1) avg
  - Iteration: O(n + capacity)
concepts.ops_advanced:
- Universal hashing: guarantees O(1) expected independent of key distribution
  - Robin Hood hashing: reduces variance by swapping rich entries for poor ones
  - Cuckoo hashing: worst-case O(1) lookup via two hash functions and guaranteed placement
concepts.complexity_note: O(n) worst case occurs with all keys hashing to same bucket — prevented by good hash functions and load factor control
concepts.amortized_note: Resize doubles capacity when alpha > threshold; O(n) rehash cost amortized over n inserts = O(1) per insert
concepts.beginner_use_case: counting word frequencies in a document
concepts.beginner_reason: each word is a key; O(1) average insert and lookup makes counting fast regardless of dictionary size
concepts.applications: language runtime dictionaries (Python dict), database hash joins, caches, symbol tables, sets
concepts.intermediate_reason: O(1) average all ops makes it the fastest general-purpose associative structure
concepts.advanced_scenario: a database join between two tables of 100M rows using a hash join
concepts.advanced_reason: hash table build phase O(n) + probe phase O(m) outperforms sort-merge join O(n log n + m log m) for unsorted data
concepts.real_world: Python dict (compact hash table), Java HashMap, Redis hash type, PostgreSQL hash join
concepts.advantages:
O(1) average insert/lookup/delete
Versatile (used as set, map, cache)
O(n) hash join
concepts.disadvantages:
O(n) worst case without good hashing
No ordered iteration
Memory overhead from empty slots
concepts.prefer_this: O(1) average ops are needed and order doesn't matter
concepts.prefer_alt: sorted order, range queries, or worst-case guarantees are required
concepts.key_diff: Hash table is O(1) average but unordered; BST is O(log n) but sorted and range-queryable
concepts.limitations: worst-case O(n) without good hash function; no ordering; hash DoS attacks if hash is predictable
concepts.mitigations: use SipHash (Rust, Python 3.4+) to resist hash-flooding attacks; keep load factor < 0.75
concepts.prod_example: Python 3.6+ dict uses a compact hash table with insertion-order preservation

concepts.name: Bloom Filter
concepts.definition: a probabilistic data structure using a bit array and multiple hash functions to test set membership, with no false negatives but a tunable false positive rate.
concepts.analogy: a quick security check that might wave through an innocent person occasionally, but never stops someone who definitely belongs
concepts.basic_ops: add(x), contains(x) -> {definitely not in set, probably in set}
concepts.internal_layout: a bit array of size m; k independent hash functions map each element to k bit positions
concepts.memory_note: m bits total — far less than storing elements; m = -n ln(p) / (ln 2)^2 for desired FP rate p
concepts.struct_props: O(k) insert and query (k = number of hash functions); no false negatives; tunable false positive rate
concepts.invariants: once a bit is set it stays set; no deletion (standard); false positive rate epsilon ~= (1 - e^{-kn/m})^k
concepts.theoretical_note: Optimal k = (m/n) ln 2 hash functions minimizes false positive rate for given m/n ratio
concepts.ops_basic:
- Add: O(k)
  - Contains: O(k)
  - False positive rate: epsilon = (1 - e^{-kn/m})^k
concepts.ops_full:
- Add: O(k) — set k bit positions
  - Contains: O(k) — check k bit positions (all must be 1)
  - Delete: not supported (standard)
  - Union: O(m) bitwise OR
concepts.ops_advanced:
- Optimal m = -n ln(epsilon) / (ln 2)^2 bits for n elements at FP rate epsilon
  - Counting Bloom filter: replace bits with counters to support deletion
  - Cuckoo filter: supports deletion with better FP rate at same space
concepts.space_complexity: O(m) bits where m ~= 9.6 bits per element for 1% FP rate
concepts.complexity_note: At 1% FP rate, Bloom filter uses ~9.6 bits/element vs ~64 bits for a hash set entry — 6.7x smaller
concepts.amortized_note: All ops are exactly O(k) — no resizing or amortization (fixed size)
concepts.beginner_use_case: checking if a URL has been visited before in a web crawler
concepts.beginner_reason: Bloom filter uses tiny memory and quickly rules out unvisited URLs; rare false positives just cause a redundant visit
concepts.applications: database query optimization (skip disk reads for missing keys), CDN cache membership, spell checkers, network routers
concepts.intermediate_reason: eliminates expensive disk reads or network lookups for elements definitely not in a set
concepts.advanced_scenario: a database avoids reading disk pages for keys that don't exist (LSM tree compaction)
concepts.advanced_reason: Bloom filter per SSTable in LevelDB/RocksDB eliminates >99% of unnecessary disk reads for missing keys
concepts.real_world: RocksDB/LevelDB per-SSTable Bloom filters, Google Bigtable, Apache Cassandra read path, Chrome safe-browsing
concepts.advantages:
Extremely memory-efficient
O(k) ops regardless of set size
No false negatives
concepts.disadvantages:
False positives (tunable but unavoidable)
No deletion (standard variant)
Cannot retrieve elements
concepts.prefer_this: memory is constrained and false positives are acceptable
concepts.prefer_alt: exact membership is required or elements need to be retrieved
concepts.key_diff: Bloom filter uses ~10 bits/element with small FP rate; hash set uses ~100+ bits/element but is exact
concepts.limitations: false positives; no deletion; not resizable without full rebuild
concepts.mitigations: use Counting Bloom filter for deletion support; use Cuckoo filter for better FP/space tradeoff
concepts.prod_example: RocksDB places a Bloom filter per SSTable so point reads can skip 99% of disk seeks for missing keys

concepts.name: Trie
concepts.definition: a tree where each node represents a character, and paths from root to nodes represent prefixes of stored strings.
concepts.analogy: a phone book organized letter-by-letter — all names starting with A share the first branch, Ab share the first two, and so on
concepts.internal_layout: nodes = {children[26] or HashMap<char, node>, is_end}; path = sequence of characters
concepts.memory_note: O(total_characters) nodes; array-backed children use O(26*n) memory; hash-backed children save space
concepts.struct_props: O(L) insert/search/prefix-search where L = string length; independent of number of stored strings
concepts.invariants: root is empty; each edge represents one character; is_end marks complete word nodes
concepts.theoretical_note: Trie search complexity O(L) is independent of n — crucial for large dictionaries where L << log n
concepts.ops_basic:
- Insert: O(L)
  - Search: O(L)
  - Prefix search: O(L + k) where k = matches
concepts.ops_full:
- Insert/search/delete: O(L)
  - Prefix listing: O(L + k)
  - Longest prefix match: O(L)
  - Autocomplete: O(L + k)
concepts.ops_advanced:
- Compressed trie (Patricia): merges single-child chains into one edge — O(n) nodes instead of O(total_chars)
  - Ternary search trie: memory-efficient with <, =, > children
  - Suffix trie/suffix array: enables O(L) substring search across a text
concepts.space_complexity: O(total_characters) up to O(26n) for array-backed
concepts.complexity_note: Array-backed children: O(26) per node (fast); hash-backed: O(actual_children) per node (space-efficient)
concepts.amortized_note: All ops are exactly O(L) — no amortization; path length bounds everything
concepts.beginner_use_case: autocomplete in a search box — suggest completions as the user types
concepts.beginner_reason: trie prefix search finds all words starting with a prefix in O(L + k) — naturally supports autocomplete
concepts.applications: autocomplete, spell checkers, IP routing (longest prefix match), DNA sequence search, text compression (LZ78)
concepts.intermediate_reason: O(L) ops independent of dictionary size — outperforms hash table when prefix queries are needed
concepts.advanced_scenario: a router must find the longest-matching prefix among 800,000 IP prefixes in nanoseconds
concepts.advanced_reason: compressed trie (Patricia tree) enables O(32) worst-case longest-prefix match for IPv4 — used in every router
concepts.real_world: Linux kernel FIB trie for IP routing, Elasticsearch FST for inverted index, Redis RESP3 command lookup
concepts.advantages:
O(L) ops independent of n
Native prefix search
Memory sharing for common prefixes
concepts.disadvantages:
O(26n) memory for array-backed
Can be slower than hash table for exact lookups
Complex implementation
concepts.prefer_this: prefix queries, autocomplete, or longest-prefix match are needed
concepts.prefer_alt: only exact-match lookup is needed — hash table is simpler and faster
concepts.key_diff: Trie enables prefix queries in O(L); hash table supports only exact match in O(L) but with lower constant
concepts.limitations: high memory for sparse character sets; array-backed wastes O(26) per node for small alphabets
concepts.mitigations: use hash-backed or compressed trie (Patricia) for space efficiency; radix tree for IP routing
concepts.prod_example: Linux kernel uses a LC-trie (level-compressed trie) for O(1) average IP route lookup

concepts.name: Cuckoo Hash Table
concepts.definition: a hash table using two hash functions and two tables where every key has exactly two possible positions, achieving worst-case O(1) lookup.
concepts.analogy: a cuckoo bird that kicks out existing eggs — if your spot is taken, you displace the occupant, who must find their alternate spot
concepts.basic_ops: insert (with displacement), lookup (check two positions), delete (clear one position)
concepts.internal_layout: two arrays T1 and T2; key x goes to T1[h1(x)] or T2[h2(x)]; insert displaces existing key cyclically
concepts.memory_note: load factor capped at ~50% to avoid infinite displacement loops; ~2n slots for n keys
concepts.struct_props: worst-case O(1) lookup (always exactly 2 positions), O(1) amortized insert, O(1) delete
concepts.invariants: each key occupies exactly one of two positions; no chaining; lookup checks exactly 2 slots
concepts.theoretical_note: Pagh and Rodler (2004) proved O(1) worst-case lookup with O(1) amortized insert if load factor < 50%
concepts.ops_basic:
- Lookup: O(1) worst case (2 checks)
  - Insert: O(1) amortized
  - Delete: O(1)
concepts.ops_full:
- Lookup: O(1) worst case
  - Insert: O(1) amortized (rare rehash if cycle detected)
  - Delete: O(1) — just clear the slot
  - Rehash: O(n) when cycle detected
concepts.ops_advanced:
- Cycle detection during insert triggers O(n) rehash with new hash functions
  - d-cuckoo: d > 2 tables reduce rehash probability dramatically
  - Cuckoo filter: uses cuckoo hashing for probabilistic set membership with deletion support
concepts.complexity_note: Lookup is always exactly 2 array accesses — true O(1) worst case, unlike chaining which is O(1) only average
concepts.amortized_note: Rehash is O(n) but expected to happen with probability O(1/n^2) — amortized cost per insert is O(1)
concepts.beginner_use_case: a lookup table where guaranteed constant-time reads are critical (e.g., packet classification)
concepts.beginner_reason: cuckoo hashing guarantees at most 2 memory accesses per lookup — no worst-case linear chain
concepts.applications: network packet classification, hardware CAM (Content Addressable Memory), cache lookup tables
concepts.intermediate_reason: true O(1) worst-case lookup (not just average) is essential in latency-critical systems
concepts.advanced_scenario: a line-rate network switch must classify 100M packets/sec, each requiring a table lookup in less than 10ns
concepts.advanced_reason: cuckoo hash's guaranteed 2-probe lookup maps to exactly 2 DRAM accesses — predictable and parallelizable in hardware
concepts.real_world: Intel DPDK rte_hash uses cuckoo hashing for O(1) worst-case packet flow table lookup
concepts.advantages:
Worst-case O(1) lookup (exactly 2 probes)
O(1) delete
Cache-friendly (2 fixed memory locations)
concepts.disadvantages:
50% max load factor (2x memory)
Rehash on cycle detection
Complex insert logic
concepts.prefer_this: worst-case lookup latency must be bounded (real-time, hardware, networking)
concepts.prefer_alt: high load factor is needed and average-case O(1) is acceptable
concepts.key_diff: Cuckoo gives worst-case O(1) lookup; chaining gives average O(1) but potentially O(n) worst case
concepts.limitations: 50% load cap wastes memory; rare but O(n) rehash; more complex than open addressing
concepts.mitigations: use d > 2 hash functions to push load factor to 90%+ while keeping rehash probability negligible
concepts.prod_example: DPDK rte_hash (used in major cloud providers' virtual switches) uses 4-way cuckoo hashing for sub-microsecond flow lookups

concepts.name: Adjacency List
concepts.definition: a graph representation where each vertex stores a list of its neighboring vertices (and optionally edge weights).
